As in DeiT, the MLP expansion ratio is 4. In the subsampling layers we use N = 4C/D = 16,24, respectively. B Visualizations: attention bias The attention bias maps from Eqn. 1 in the main paper are just two-dimensional maps. Therefore we can vizualize them, see Figure 6. They can be read as the amount of attention between two pixels that are at a certain relative position. The lowest values of the bias are low enough (- 20) to suppress the attention between the two pixels, since they are input to a softmax. We can observe that some heads are quite uniform, while other heads specialize in nearby pixels ( e.g. most heads of the shrinking attention). Some are clearly direc- tional, e.g. heads 1 and 4 of Stage 2/block 1 handle the pixels adjacent vertically and horizontally (respectively). Head 1 of stage 2, block 4 has a speciﬁc period-2 pattern that may be due to the fact that its output is fed to a sub- sampling ﬁlter in the next shrinking attention block. 15Stage 1, attention block 1 (ﬁrst one) head 0  head 1  head 2  head 3 ... Stage 1, attention block 4 head 0  head 1  head 2  head 3 Shrinking attention between stages 1 and 2 head 0  head 1  head 2  head 3  head 4  head 5  head 6  head 7 Stage 2, attention block 1 head 0  head 1  head 2  head 3  head 4  head 5 ... Stage 2, attention block 4 head 0  head 1  head 2  head 3  head 4  head 5 ... Stage 3, attention block 4 (last one) head 0  head 1  head 2  head 3  head 4  head 5  head 6  head 7 Figure 6: Visualization of the attention bias for several blocks of a trained LeViT-256 model. The center for which the attention is computed is the upper left pixel of the map (with a square). Higher bias values are in yellow, lower values in dark blue (values range from -20 to 7). 16

new_method.method:
Title: DASH-HiLo-Anchor for SHViT: Static-shape co-budgeting of channels, tokens, and late HR cues under single-head constraints

Motivation
- Gap in SHViT: The current Pareto is bounded by (i) fixed, prefix-based partial-channel use that ignores per-stage/channel utility, (ii) quadratic token costs inside SHSA even when only a partial channel slice is attended, and (iii) stride-16 macro that weakens small-object/detail sensitivity. Prior accelerators (ToMe/TPS/Zero-TPrune/SViTE) either alter global token length, rely on multi-head/LN-heavy changes, or introduce dynamic control flow that harms SHViT’s static-shape, BN/Conv fusion and CoreML/ONNX deployment.
- Why it matters: SHViT’s appeal on mobile/edge hinges on (a) single-head simplicity, (b) static tensor shapes at block interfaces, and (c) BN/Conv fusion. A method that shifts the Pareto while preserving these properties enables predictable latency, exportability, and multi-mode deployment (server/CPU/mobile) from a single checkpoint.

Methodology
Core idea: Add three pluggable, static-shape modules that only modify SHSA.forward and add one late fusion site. All block I/O remains [B,C,H,W]; no heads are added; no global sequence change. Export 2–3 fixed budget modes per checkpoint with frozen indices and compile-time shapes.

A) DASHViT: Dual-Axis Static co-budgeting inside SHSA (channels + tokens)
- Channel co-budgeting (learned, frozen at inference)
  - Overprovision pdim_eff (>pdim) during training on the SHSA path; insert a tiny scorer Conv1×1(pdim_eff→pdim_eff) + GAP to rank channels; straight-through Gumbel-TopK selects pdim channels. After warmup, freeze TopK indices per stage (tie across blocks by default) and store as Buffers. Inference uses static gather/scatter along channel dim → constant graphs and preserved shapes.
  - Stabilizers: (i) channel-coverage/diversity loss on selection histograms, (ii) index-dropout 5–10% during warmup, (iii) small L2 on pre_norm outputs to keep BN stats stable post-freeze. Optionally distill low-budget modes from max-acc mode.
- SHSA-internal token merging at fixed N′ (bounded inside the partial-channel branch)
  - Workflow (within SHSA only): after gather of selected channels, compute Q/K/V; build a bipartite pairing over tokens using K-similarity; fuse V via size-weighted averaging; add log(token_size) to attention logits; attend at reduced N′; scatter back to the H×W grid; concatenate with the bypass channels; apply existing 1×1 proj. N′ is fixed per mode → static shapes.
  - Backends per export target: (i) ONNX/server: bipartite matching with constant shapes (Gather/ScatterElements, opset ≥13). (ii) CoreML/mobile: deterministic Morton-order window pairing with pre-baked index maps per stage/resolution; identical graphs across inputs.
- Budgets (examples, S4@256): fast (pdim≈0.18C, r_merge≈0.35N), balanced (≈0.22C, ≈0.20N), max-acc (≈0.26C, r_merge=0). Multi-mode training: sample 2 modes/batch; consistency loss on pre-proj logits.
- Novelty vs ToMe/TPS/Zero-TPrune/SViTE: Token operations are confined to the partial-channel SHSA branch, then scattered back; block interfaces and norms unchanged; indices are learned once then frozen into static gathers; no variable-length sequences or multi-head reworks; no training-time sparsity masks.

B) HiLo-SHSA: Frequency-aware attention within the single-head, partial-channel path
- Split the attended channels (pdim) into Hi/Lo groups by ratio α per stage using the same qkv/proj, no extra norms:
  - Hi-Fi: masked local window attention (s×s, e.g., s=2@256, s=4/6@≥384) to capture detail at low cost.
  - Lo-Fi: average-pool K,V over s×s windows (length N/s²); keep Q at full N; compute global attention and upscatter V′ to original grid. Reduces quadratic cost by ≈1/s² while keeping long-range context.
- Concatenate outputs along channels and reuse the existing 1×1 proj. Shapes and BN fusion intact.
- Differs from HiLo/LITv2: applied inside SHViT’s single-head, partial-channel path; no multi-head head-splitting or LN-heavy redesign; preserves all SHViT block interfaces.

C) Sparse HR-Anchor Fusion (late, once per network)
- Select K salient high-res anchors at stage-1 using a cheap saliency (local variance or Sobel energy); extract 7×7 HR crops via pixel-unshuffle; process with a tiny DWConv micro-tower to pdim_a channels (~5–10% of C, width=min(0.25C,48)).
- Fuse once after the last SHSA block via single-head cross-attention from stage-3 tokens (Q) to anchors (K,V). Complexity O(N·K), K≪N. Residual-scale the fused slice. Curriculum: start random anchors → anneal to saliency top-K.
- Rationale: recovers small-object/detail cues without multi-branch pyramids or shape changes.

Implementation notes (built on the provided SHViT code)
- Modify only model/shvit.py: SHSA.forward to host DASHViT and HiLo-SHSA; add one HR-Anchor module before the head. Keep BasicBlock/FFN/PatchMerging unchanged.
- New components: channel scorer (Conv1×1+GAP, buffers for frozen TopK); in-branch token merge utils (matching, size-weighted V, log-size logits, scatter-back); HiLo masking/avg-pool/reshape ops; HR-Anchor saliency, micro-tower, cross-attn.
- Static export: per-mode artifacts with frozen channel indices and fixed N′. ONNX opset ≥13 (Gather/ScatterElements). CoreML uses deterministic folding with pre-tiled index maps per resolution. Provide a tiny runtime dispatcher to choose an artifact per device budget.
- Training recipe: 2-phase. Phase-1 (150–200 ep): learn channel indices with ST Gumbel-TopK; sample 2 modes/batch; inter-mode consistency 0.02–0.03; index-dropout; coverage/diversity regularization. Phase-2 (100–150 ep): freeze indices and per-mode budgets; optional KD for fast/balanced from max-acc (DeiT hard-KD); short BN recalibration per mode.
- Defaults (S4@256): DASH budgets {(0.22C,0.2N),(0.18C,0.35N),(0.26C,0)}; HiLo α stage2=0.6, stage3=0.7; s=2@256, s=4/6@≥384; HR-Anchor K=8 (cls), 16 (det/seg); pdim_a≈0.05C.

Why clearly novel vs prior work
- SHViT: co-budgets channels and tokens within SHSA while preserving single-head, static block I/O; adds late sparse HR fusion without altering the macro.
- ToMe/TPS/Zero-TPrune: do not confine token ops to a partial-channel single-head branch with scatter-back; typically change global sequence length or require dynamic control flow. Our indices/budgets are frozen per mode → constant graphs for ONNX/CoreML.
- HiLo/LITv2: we do frequency splitting inside the partial single-head path, reusing SHSA’s qkv/proj and norms; no head-splitting, extra LNs, or new heads.
- SViTE/S2ViTE: no model sparsity or mask-based training; budgets are static and export-time fixed, keeping BN/Conv fusion and SHViT deployment strengths.
- DeiT/OmniMAE/LaViT: orthogonal training/pretraining/distillation ideas; our contribution is a deployment-safe micro-architectural efficiency upgrade tailored to SHViT.

Expected impact
- Pareto shift: 15–35% SHSA FLOPs reduction in fast/balanced modes at equal or better Top-1 on ImageNet via smarter channel routing and intra-SHSA token merging; HiLo-SHSA further trims quadratic cost with stronger locality; HR-Anchor recovers small-object sensitivity with only 3–5% extra FLOPs.
- Deployment: Maintains SHViT virtues—static shapes, BN/Conv fusion, single-head simplicity. A single checkpoint exports multiple fixed-budget variants for server/CPU/mobile; ONNX/CoreML friendly (no control flow; Gather/ScatterElements or deterministic folding).
- Applications: Mobile AR/robotics/drones requiring predictable latency and better small-object recognition; server-side autoscaling with budget-aware dispatch. Broader use as a template for single-head, static-graph efficient Transformers in edge stacks.

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: Channel index overfitting or instability after freezing. Mitigate with coverage/diversity regularization, index-dropout, and BN recalibration per exported mode.
- Risk: Matching overhead on mobile. Mitigate by deterministic Morton folding with precomputed maps; avoid data-dependent control flow on CoreML.
- Risk: HR-Anchor mis-selection. Mitigate with curriculum (random→saliency), low K, and residual scaling.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Experiment 1 — ImageNet Pareto shift and ablations (channels + tokens + HiLo + anchors)
Objective/Hypotheses
- Validate that DASH-HiLo-Anchor shifts the accuracy–efficiency Pareto while preserving static shapes and single-head constraints.
- Test each component and their combination: A) channel co-budgeting, B) SHSA-internal token merge, C) HiLo-SHSA, D) Sparse HR-Anchor.

Datasets
- ImageNet-1k (train: 1.28M, val: 50k). Resolution: 256 short side (primary), optional 384 for scaling experiments.

Models/Variants
- Baseline: SHViT-S4@256 (unaltered single-head).
- A1: +Channel co-budgeting only (DASHViT channels; no token merge, no HiLo, no anchors).
- A2: +Channels + internal token merge (fixed N′) inside SHSA.
- A3: +Channels + token merge + HiLo-SHSA.
- A4 (Full): A3 + Sparse HR-Anchor fusion once near the head.
- External comparators (optional): ToMe or TPS on the full head, matched to similar token reduction ratios for context (acknowledging they break static shapes). Use default public PyTorch repos to integrate.

Budgets/Modes (frozen per exported artifact)
- fast: (pdim≈0.18C, r_merge≈0.35N)
- balanced: (pdim≈0.22C, r_merge≈0.20N)
- max-acc: (pdim≈0.26C, r_merge=0)
- HiLo ratios: α stage2=0.6, stage3=0.7; local window s=2 for 256-res.
- Anchors: K=8, pdim_a≈0.05C; residual scale 0.5.

Training Protocol (PyTorch)
- Optim: AdamW, base LR 3e-4 (256 batch effective), weight decay 0.05, cosine LR schedule, 300–350 total epochs.
- Aug: RandAug(9,0.5), random resize-crop to 256, Mixup 0.2, CutMix 0.2, label smoothing 0.1.
- Phase-1 (150–200 ep):
  - Train channel scorer Conv1×1 with ST Gumbel-TopK. Overprovision pdim_eff≈1.5×pdim.
  - Sample 2 budget modes per batch; add inter-mode consistency loss (0.02–0.03) on pre-proj logits.
  - Regularizers: coverage/diversity on selection histograms (entropy + per-channel usage variance target), index-dropout 5–10%, small L2 on pre_norm outputs for BN stability.
- Freeze: pick TopK channel indices per stage from EMA of selections; store as buffers.
- Phase-2 (100–150 ep):
  - Freeze indices and per-mode N′; train heads/blocks end-to-end; optional KD for fast/balanced from max-acc (DeiT-style hard distill, weight 0.5).
  - Short BN recalibration per mode: run 1–2k batches without weight updates.

Inference/Export
- Export three static artifacts (fast/balanced/max-acc) per checkpoint.
- ONNX opset ≥13 with Gather/ScatterElements for channel selection and token scatter-back.
- For mobile, use deterministic Morton window pairing with precomputed index maps to avoid data-dependent control flow.

Metrics
- Accuracy: Top-1 on ImageNet-1k val.
- Efficiency: FLOPs (ptflops/fvcore), params, peak memory, throughput (images/s), latency (p50/p95) on RTX-class GPU and 8-core CPU.
- Static-shape deployability: ONNX export success, CoreML/ORT compile success, number of dynamic graph ops (expect 0), latency variance across 100 random inputs (expect low variance).
- Component ablations: delta accuracy and FLOPs per added component (A1→A2→A3→A4).

Implementation Notes
- Modify only SHSA.forward for A1–A3; add one HR-Anchor module before classifier head.
- Token merge: build pairing by K-similarity (server) or Morton pairing (mobile). After V fusion and attention at N′, scatter back to H×W and concat with bypass channels; reuse existing 1×1 proj.
- HiLo: split pdim by α; Hi: masked local window attention; Lo: avg-pool K,V by s×s; Q at full N; upscatter V′.
- Anchors: Sobel- or local-variance-based per-pixel saliency at stage-1; pixel-unshuffle 7×7 crops; DWConv micro-tower; single-head cross-attn from stage-3 tokens.

Success Criteria (expected)
- At matched accuracy, ≥15–35% SHSA FLOPs reduction vs baseline; or at matched latency, +0.3–0.8 Top-1.
- A2 outperforms A1 at the same FLOPs; A3 adds +0.2–0.4 Top-1 at small extra cost; A4 recovers small-object errors with ≤5% extra FLOPs.
- All three modes export and run with constant graph shapes; latency variance ≤3% across inputs.


Experiment 2 — Small-object/detail sensitivity probes (HR-Anchor + HiLo)
Objective/Hypotheses
- Quantify gains on small objects and fine detail: HR-Anchor should recover detail lost by stride-16; HiLo should improve locality at low cost.

Datasets/Probes
- ImageNet-1k ShrinkPad probe: for each val image, downscale the full image by r∈{1.0, 0.6, 0.4, 0.25} and center-pad back to 256×256 with mean pixel value. This reduces effective object size without requiring bounding boxes.
- COCO-Cls-Small (optional): derive a small-object classification set from COCO 2017:
  - From annotations, select boxes with shorter side in [16, 64] px in the original image.
  - Paste each box on a 256×256 canvas at random location with background mean; label by category (80-way). Train split from train2017, eval on val2017.

Models/Variants
- Baseline SHViT-S4@256.
- +HiLo only (A3 minus anchors).
- +HR-Anchor only (baseline + anchors; keeps single-head SHSA unchanged).
- Full (A4).
- Use balanced mode budgets for all, with frozen indices and N′ as in Exp 1.

Training
- For ImageNet ShrinkPad: fine-tune each model 30–50 epochs on ImageNet-1k with r sampled uniformly from {1.0, 0.6, 0.4, 0.25} per sample (data augmentation), keeping budgets frozen.
- For COCO-Cls-Small: train 50 epochs from ImageNet-pretrained backbone with smaller LR (1e-4), same augmentations but no Mixup/CutMix.
- Anchor curriculum: start with random K for 5 epochs, then anneal to saliency top-K by epoch 15.

Metrics
- Top-1 vs r on ImageNet ShrinkPad; report accuracy drop ΔAcc(r)=Acc(r)−Acc(1.0). Expect smaller drops for +HR-Anchor and Full.
- On COCO-Cls-Small: mAcc@top-1; per-size-bin accuracy (16–32, 32–48, 48–64 px); confusion matrices for tiny objects.
- Qualitative sanity checks: visualize 64 random images with overlayed selected anchors and attention heatmaps; report fraction of anchors intersecting high-variance regions; attention mass within 32×32 windows around anchors.

Implementation Notes
- ShrinkPad transform: standard torchvision resize with bicubic, then paste into zero-mean or dataset-mean padded canvas.
- HR-Anchor saliency: torch Sobel filters or local variance via avgpool; threshold and top-K by score.
- Pixel-unshuffle to produce HR token crops; ensure shapes remain static by fixing K and crop size.

Success Criteria (expected)
- At r=0.4: Full model improves Top-1 by +1.0–1.8 over baseline; HiLo-only gives +0.3–0.6; HR-Anchor-only gives +0.7–1.2.
- On COCO-Cls-Small: +2–4 mAcc absolute over baseline, most gains in the 16–32 px bin.


Experiment 3 — Portability, backend swap, and index robustness
Objective/Hypotheses
- Demonstrate deployment safety: static-shape export to ONNX/CoreML with predictable latency across inputs and backends.
- Show that deterministic Morton pairing (mobile) closely matches K-sim bipartite pairing (server) in accuracy.
- Validate robustness of frozen channel indices across dataset shifts and runs; confirm BN recalibration effects.

Backends
- Server backend: K-sim bipartite matching for token merge (constant-size tensors; no control-flow ops), exported to ONNX.
- Mobile backend: deterministic Morton-order window pairing with precomputed index maps per stage/resolution; exported to CoreML.

Protocol
- Train Full model (A4) using server backend as in Exp 1.
- Inference-only swap: replace token merge with Morton backend; keep the same frozen channel indices, N′, K. Evaluate on all probes.
- Export both backends:
  - ONNX opset ≥13 for server; verify absence of dynamic ops (scan graph to ensure Gather/ScatterElements with constant indices).
  - CoreML Tools convert mobile backend; compile on macOS/iOS; run on-device (Metal) if available, otherwise CoreML CPU fallback.
- Latency predictability: run 1k random ImageNet val images on each backend; record p50/p95 latency and variance; check that graph shapes are constant for all inputs.
- Dataset shift robustness: evaluate all three modes on ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-Sketch. For each, test with and without BN recalibration (1k batches) and report deltas.
- Index stability across seeds: train Phase-1 three times with different seeds; compute Jaccard overlap of per-stage TopK channel sets; measure accuracy variance of exported modes.
- Ablate regularizers: rerun Phase-1 without coverage loss and without index-dropout; compare index stability and downstream accuracy after freezing.

Metrics
- Accuracy deltas between backends (server vs mobile) per mode: expect ≤0.3% Top-1 absolute.
- Export success rate (pass/fail), model size, compile time, runtime latency (p50/p95), and latency variance (<3%).
- Cross-dataset drops: ΔAcc ≤ baseline’s drops; BN recalibration recovers ≥30–60% of the loss due to freezing.
- Jaccard overlap of selected channels across seeds: ≥0.8 per stage with regularizers; lower without them.

Implementation Notes
- Provide a simple registry to swap merge backends at inference while preserving identical tensor shapes.
- Use onnx-simplifier and Netron to verify constant shapes; for CoreML, fold precomputed indices into constant tensors.
- For timing: use torch.cuda.Event on GPU, time.perf_counter on CPU; warm up 100 iters, then average over 300 iters; pin memory; set torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark=False for determinism when measuring variance.

Overall Deliverables
- Training scripts (phase-1 and phase-2) that parameterize budgets and freeze indices; export scripts per mode.
- Evaluation harness producing: accuracy/FLOPs/latency tables; ShrinkPad curves; backend accuracy parity; latency variance reports; index stability plots.
- Minimal visualizations to sanity-check anchors and attention.

Feasibility
- All components are implementable in PyTorch with standard ops (Conv1×1, Gather/ScatterElements, avgpool, unfold/pixel-unshuffle). Exports via torch.onnx and coremltools. No dynamic control flow is required.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
Title: Experimental plan for DASH-HiLo-Anchor for SHViT: static-shape co-budgeting of channels, tokens, and late HR anchors under single-head constraints

Overview
- We implement and evaluate DASH-HiLo-Anchor within a standard SHViT-S4 backbone, modifying only SHSA.forward and adding one late HR-Anchor fusion. All block I/O remains [B,C,H,W] and the model stays single-head.
- We train in two phases to learn and then freeze channel indices and per-mode token-merge budgets. We export three static artifacts (fast/balanced/max-acc) with fixed shapes.
- We run three experiments:
  1) ImageNet Pareto and ablations (A1→A4)
  2) Small-object/detail probes (ShrinkPad, optional COCO-Cls-Small)
  3) Portability/backends/robustness (ONNX/CoreML, K-sim vs Morton pairing, index stability)
- We emphasize reuse: a single phase-1/2 training produces all variants and artifacts; experiments share checkpoints, dataloaders, logging, FLOPs/latency harnesses.

Common Infrastructure
- Libraries: torch>=2.1, torchvision, timm, fvcore or ptflops, torchmetrics, onnx, onnxsim, onnxruntime, coremltools (for macOS/iOS tests), numpy, pandas, matplotlib/seaborn, scipy (for Hungarian, optional), einops.
- Baseline: SHViT-S4 reference code (single-head self-attention per block). We assume model/shvit.py exposes SHSA; BasicBlock/FFN/PatchMerging unchanged.
- Config registry: YAML or argparse flags to set budgets per mode, HiLo ratios, s, anchors K, pdim_a, backend type (server K-sim vs mobile Morton), and export toggles.
- Reproducibility: seed all RNGs; cudnn.deterministic=True and cudnn.benchmark=False during measurements; use torch.set_float32_matmul_precision("high") during training.

Core modules (implementation sketches)
1) Channel scorer with ST Gumbel-TopK and freezeable indices

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from einops import rearrange

class ChannelScorer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, c_eff, temperature=1.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.score_conv = nn.Conv2d(c_eff, c_eff, kernel_size=1, bias=True)
        self.register_buffer('frozen_idx', None, persistent=True)
        self.tau = temperature

    @torch.no_grad()
    def freeze_indices(self, hist_scores, k):
        # hist_scores: [stages][C_eff] EMA or averaged scores
        self.frozen_idx = torch.topk(hist_scores, k, dim=-1).indices

    def forward(self, x, pdim, training_mode=True, index_dropout=0.0):
        # x: [B, C_eff, H, W]
        B, C_eff, H, W = x.shape
        if (not training_mode) and (self.frozen_idx is not None):
            idx = self.frozen_idx
            x_sel = torch.index_select(x, 1, idx)  # [B, pdim, H, W]
            return x_sel, idx
        # train-time scoring
        s = self.score_conv(x)  # [B, C_eff, H, W]
        s = F.adaptive_avg_pool2d(s, 1).squeeze(-1).squeeze(-1)  # [B, C_eff]
        # ST Gumbel-TopK
        g = -torch.empty_like(s).exponential_().log()  # Gumbel(0,1)
        s_g = (s + g) / max(self.tau, 1e-4)
        topk = torch.topk(s_g, k=pdim, dim=-1)
        idx = topk.indices  # [B, pdim]
        # index dropout: randomly swap out some indices with random channels
        if index_dropout > 0 and training_mode:
            mask = (torch.rand_like(idx.float()) < index_dropout)
            rand_idx = torch.randint(low=0, high=C_eff, size=idx.shape, device=idx.device)
            idx = torch.where(mask, rand_idx, idx)
        # Straight-through gather: gather per-sample then average to share across batch
        x_sel = gather_channels_batchwise(x, idx)  # [B, pdim, H, W]
        return x_sel, idx

def gather_channels_batchwise(x, idx):
    # x: [B, C, H, W], idx: [B, K]
    B, C, H, W = x.shape
    k = idx.shape[-1]
    idx_exp = idx.unsqueeze(-1).unsqueeze(-1).expand(B, k, H, W)
    # Build base: [B, C, H, W] -> [B, C, HW]
    x_flat = x.view(B, C, -1)
    out = torch.gather(x_flat, 1, idx.unsqueeze(-1).expand(B, k, H*W))
    out = out.view(B, k, H, W)
    return out
```

Coverage/diversity regularizer and EMA of per-channel usage

```python
class ChannelUsageEMA:
    def __init__(self, C_eff, momentum=0.9, device='cpu'):
        self.m = momentum
        self.hist = torch.zeros(C_eff, device=device)

    @torch.no_grad()
    def update(self, batch_indices, C_eff):
        # batch_indices: [B, K]
        onehot = torch.zeros(C_eff, device=self.hist.device)
        vals, counts = batch_indices.flatten().unique(return_counts=True)
        onehot[vals] = counts.float()
        onehot = onehot / (batch_indices.numel() + 1e-6)
        self.hist = self.m * self.hist + (1 - self.m) * onehot

    def reg(self, target_var=1.0/C_eff, entropy_w=1e-3):
        # Encourage spread: high entropy and low variance toward uniform
        p = self.hist.clamp_min(1e-6)
        ent = - (p * p.log()).sum()
        var = ((p - p.mean())**2).mean()
        return -entropy_w * ent + (var - target_var).abs()
```

2) SHSA forward with DASHViT (channels + token merge) and HiLo-SHSA

We add: channel selection, in-branch token merge, HiLo split (local Hi, pooled global Lo), and then concatenate with the bypass channels and reuse the existing 1x1 projection.

```python
class SHSA_DASH_HiLo(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, C, pdim_eff, pdim_budget, nhead=1, hilo_alpha=0.6, win_size=2,
                 server_backend=True, N_reduced_ratio=0.2, frozen_token_maps=None):
        super().__init__()
        assert nhead == 1
        self.C = C
        self.pdim_eff = pdim_eff
        self.pdim_budget = pdim_budget
        self.hilo_alpha = hilo_alpha
        self.win = win_size
        self.server_backend = server_backend
        self.r_merge = N_reduced_ratio
        self.frozen_token_maps = frozen_token_maps  # for mobile Morton
        # qkv/proj on full C, but we will only feed pdim channels into attention branch
        self.qkv = nn.Conv2d(pdim_budget, pdim_budget*3, kernel_size=1, bias=False)
        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(C, C, kernel_size=1, bias=False)  # existing proj reused
        self.scorer = ChannelScorer(c_eff=pdim_eff)
        # Optional pre-norm or BN integration assumed in outer block

    def forward(self, x, mode_cfg, training_phase=True):
        # x: [B, C, H, W]
        B, C, H, W = x.shape
        pdim = mode_cfg['pdim']
        r_merge = mode_cfg['r_merge']
        hilo_alpha = mode_cfg['hilo_alpha']
        win = mode_cfg['win']
        # 1) Select pdim channels from an overprovisioned conv-preprocessed branch
        # Assume a prior 1x1 conv increased features to pdim_eff before SHSA, or replicate via x itself if C==pdim_eff
        x_eff = x[:, :self.pdim_eff] if self.pdim_eff <= C else F.pad(x, (0,0,0,0,0,self.pdim_eff-C))
        x_sel, idx = self.scorer(x_eff, pdim, training_mode=training_phase, index_dropout=mode_cfg.get('index_dropout', 0.0))
        # Bypass channels (unchanged): the remaining C - pdim
        # For static scatter/gather at export, keep bypass as x (we'll concat after attention branch)
        # 2) QKV within selected pdim channels
        qkv = self.qkv(x_sel)
        q, k, v = torch.chunk(qkv, 3, dim=1)  # [B, pdim, H, W] each
        # 3) Token merge inside branch
        qf = rearrange(q, 'b c h w -> b (h w) c')  # [B, N, pdim]
        kf = rearrange(k, 'b c h w -> b (h w) c')
        vf = rearrange(v, 'b c h w -> b (h w) c')
        N = H*W
        N_reduced = max(1, int((1.0 - r_merge) * N))
        if r_merge > 0:
            if self.server_backend:
                merge_idx, sizes = ksim_pairing_indices(kf, N_reduced, H, W, win=win)
            else:
                # Morton pairing precomputed for given H,W,win
                merge_idx, sizes = morton_pairing_indices(H, W, N_reduced, self.frozen_token_maps)
            vf_red, kf_red, size_red = apply_merge(vf, kf, merge_idx, sizes)  # [B, N', C], [B, N', C], [B, N']
            qf_att = qf  # full length for Q
            kf_att = kf_red
            vf_att = vf_red
            attn_logits = (qf_att @ kf_att.transpose(1,2)) / (kf_att.shape[-1] ** 0.5)
            attn_logits = attn_logits + torch.log(size_red.unsqueeze(1).clamp_min(1.0))  # size prior
            attn = attn_logits.softmax(dim=-1)
            of = attn @ vf_att  # [B, N, C]
        else:
            attn_logits = (qf @ kf.transpose(1,2)) / (kf.shape[-1] ** 0.5)
            attn = attn_logits.softmax(dim=-1)
            of = attn @ vf
        o = rearrange(of, 'b (h w) c -> b c h w', h=H, w=W)
        # 4) HiLo-SHSA inside the branch: split channels of o along c
        c_hi = int(hilo_alpha * pdim)
        o_hi, o_lo = o[:, :c_hi], o[:, c_hi:]
        # Hi: local window attention refinement (masked)
        o_hi = local_window_attn(o_hi, win)
        # Lo: average-pool K,V over windows, global attention with Q at full res
        o_lo = lo_fi_global_attn(q, k, v, start_c=c_hi, win=win)
        # 5) Concatenate branch outputs and bypass channels, then existing 1x1 proj
        branch = torch.cat([o_hi, o_lo], dim=1)  # [B, pdim, H, W]
        out = torch.cat([branch, x[:, pdim:]], dim=1)  # [B, C, H, W]
        out = self.proj(out)
        return out

# Utilities

def ksim_pairing_indices(kf, N_reduced, H, W, win=2):
    # Approximate constant-shape pairing within non-overlapping windows to reduce complexity
    # kf: [B, N, C]; return merge groups and sizes for V/K fusion
    B, N, C = kf.shape
    # Partition into tiles
    kf_ = rearrange(kf, 'b (h w) c -> b h w c', h=H, w=W)
    kf_tiles = rearrange(F.unfold(rearrange(kf_, 'b h w c -> b c h w'), kernel_size=win, stride=win),
                         'b (c k) L -> b L k c', c=C, k=win*win)  # [B, ntiles, tileN, C]
    # For each tile, compute top-m prototypes by K-sim clustering-lite: pick top-mean norms and aggregate neighbors
    with torch.no_grad():
        norms = (kf_tiles**2).sum(-1)  # [B, ntiles, tileN]
        # Keep m tokens per tile such that total N' matches N_reduced
        tiles = kf_tiles.shape[1]
        tileN = kf_tiles.shape[2]
        m = max(1, int(round(N_reduced / (tiles + 1e-6))))
        m = min(m, tileN)
        sel = norms.topk(k=m, dim=-1).indices  # [B, tiles, m]
    # Build merge indices: group each token to its nearest selected prototype (cos sim)
    proto = torch.gather(kf_tiles, 2, sel.unsqueeze(-1).expand(-1,-1,-1,C))  # [B, tiles, m, C]
    sim = torch.einsum('btpc, btkc -> btpk', F.normalize(proto, dim=-1), F.normalize(kf_tiles, dim=-1))
    assign = sim.argmax(dim=2)  # [B, tiles, k]
    # Flatten groups into global token indices
    # Return simplified structures that apply_merge can process
    return (sel, assign), torch.full((B, tiles*m), fill_value=win*win/m, device=kf.device)

def apply_merge(vf, kf, merge_idx, sizes):
    # Here we provide a placeholder for server-side merge application
    # In practice we vectorize grouping by tile; for brevity we return identity when not needed
    return vf, kf, torch.ones(vf.shape[:2], device=vf.device)

def morton_pairing_indices(H, W, N_reduced, precomputed):
    # precomputed should contain a fixed mapping for (H,W) windows
    return precomputed['merge_idx'], precomputed['sizes']

def local_window_attn(x, win):
    # x: [B, C, H, W] -> masked self-attn within win x win windows
    B, C, H, W = x.shape
    pad_h = (win - H % win) % win
    pad_w = (win - W % win) % win
    x = F.pad(x, (0, pad_w, 0, pad_h))
    Hp, Wp = x.shape[-2:]
    xw = rearrange(x, 'b c (nh w1) (nw w2) -> b (nh nw) (w1 w2) c', w1=win, w2=win)
    qkv = torch.cat([xw, xw, xw], dim=-1)  # reusing features as qkv for refinement
    q, k, v = torch.chunk(qkv, 3, dim=-1)
    attn = (q @ k.transpose(-1,-2)) / (C**0.5)
    attn = attn.softmax(dim=-1)
    ow = attn @ v
    x2 = rearrange(ow, 'b t s c -> b c t s')
    x2 = rearrange(x2, 'b c (nh nw) (w1 w2) -> b c (nh w1) (nw w2)', nh=Hp//win, nw=Wp//win, w1=win, w2=win)
    return x2[..., :H, :W]

def lo_fi_global_attn(q, k, v, start_c, win=2):
    # q,k,v: [B, C, H, W]; use channels starting from start_c for Lo branch
    B, C, H, W = q.shape
    ql = q[:, start_c:]
    kl = k[:, start_c:]
    vl = v[:, start_c:]
    # Avg-pool K,V; keep Q full
    klp = F.avg_pool2d(kl, kernel_size=win, stride=win)
    vlp = F.avg_pool2d(vl, kernel_size=win, stride=win)
    qf = rearrange(ql, 'b c h w -> b (h w) c')
    kf = rearrange(klp, 'b c h w -> b (h w) c')
    vf = rearrange(vlp, 'b c h w -> b (h w) c')
    attn = (qf @ kf.transpose(1,2)) / (kf.shape[-1]**0.5)
    attn = attn.softmax(dim=-1)
    of = attn @ vf
    o = rearrange(of, 'b (h w) c -> b c h w', h=H, w=W)
    return o
```

Notes:
- The ksim_pairing_indices/apply_merge above are placeholders; in our codebase we use a vectorized tile-wise grouping to ensure constant shapes and no Python loops. For production, we optionally borrow ToMe’s grouping logic (greedy match within windows) but confine it inside the branch and fix N′ per mode.
- For mobile, we precompute Morton indices per (H,W,win) at build time and store them as buffers; shapes are constant and indices are constants in the graph.

3) Sparse HR-Anchor Fusion (late, once per network)

```python
class HRAnchorFusion(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_c_s1, pdim_a, K=8, crop=7, residual_scale=0.5, use_sobel=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.K = K
        self.crop = crop
        self.scale = residual_scale
        self.use_sobel = use_sobel
        # Micro-tower: depthwise conv -> pointwise
        self.dw = nn.Conv2d(in_c_s1, in_c_s1, kernel_size=3, padding=1, groups=in_c_s1)
        self.pw = nn.Conv2d(in_c_s1, pdim_a, kernel_size=1)
        self.cross_q = nn.Linear(pdim_a, pdim_a, bias=False)
        self.cross_k = nn.Linear(pdim_a, pdim_a, bias=False)
        self.cross_v = nn.Linear(pdim_a, pdim_a, bias=False)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(pdim_a, pdim_a, bias=False)

    def saliency(self, x):
        # x: [B, C1, H1, W1]; local variance saliency (no extra deps)
        s = x.mean(1, keepdim=True)  # [B,1,H1,W1]
        mu = F.avg_pool2d(s, 3, stride=1, padding=1)
        mu2 = F.avg_pool2d(s*s, 3, stride=1, padding=1)
        var = (mu2 - mu*mu).squeeze(1)  # [B,H1,W1]
        return var

    def extract_crops(self, x, centers):
        # centers: [B,K,2] (y,x) indices (int)
        B, C, H, W = x.shape
        pad = self.crop//2
        xp = F.pad(x, (pad,pad,pad,pad))
        crops = []
        for b in range(B):
            ys = centers[b,:,0] + pad
            xs = centers[b,:,1] + pad
            # vectorized gather by unfold
            uf = F.unfold(xp[b:b+1], kernel_size=self.crop, stride=1)  # [1, C*crop*crop, H+2p - crop +1 * W + 2p - crop +1]
            # Map (y,x) to linear index
            idx = ys*(W+2*pad - self.crop + 1) + xs
            patches = uf[0,:,idx]  # [C*crop*crop, K]
            patches = patches.transpose(0,1).contiguous().view(self.K, C, self.crop, self.crop)
            crops.append(patches)
        crops = torch.stack(crops, dim=0)  # [B, K, C, crop, crop]
        return crops

    def forward(self, feat_s1, tokens_s3):
        # feat_s1: [B, C1, H1, W1]; tokens_s3: [B, N3, pdim_a] (from stage 3 projection)
        B, C1, H1, W1 = feat_s1.shape
        sal = self.saliency(feat_s1)  # [B,H1,W1]
        # Top-K centers per image
        _, idx = torch.topk(sal.view(B, -1), k=self.K, dim=-1)
        ys = idx // W1
        xs = idx % W1
        centers = torch.stack([ys, xs], dim=-1)
        crops = self.extract_crops(feat_s1, centers)  # [B,K,C1,c,c]
        crops = crops.view(B*self.K, C1, self.crop, self.crop)
        z = self.pw(self.dw(crops))  # [B*K, pdim_a, c, c]
        z = F.adaptive_avg_pool2d(z, 1).squeeze(-1).squeeze(-1)  # [B*K, pdim_a]
        anchors = z.view(B, self.K, -1)  # [B,K,pdim_a]
        # Cross-attention: Q from tokens_s3, K/V from anchors
        Q = self.cross_q(tokens_s3)
        K = self.cross_k(anchors)
        V = self.cross_v(anchors)
        attn = (Q @ K.transpose(1,2)) / (K.shape[-1]**0.5)
        attn = attn.softmax(dim=-1)
        fused = attn @ V  # [B,N3,pdim_a]
        fused = self.proj(fused)
        return self.scale * fused
```

We insert HRAnchorFusion after the last SHSA block and before the classifier head. We project stage-3 tokens to pdim_a channels for the cross-attention slice and add residual to the token stream.

Experiment 1 — ImageNet Pareto shift and ablations (channels + tokens + HiLo + anchors)
1) Objectives/Hypotheses
- Validate that DASH-HiLo-Anchor shifts the accuracy–efficiency Pareto vs SHViT-S4@256 while preserving static shapes and single-head constraints.
- Quantify component contributions: A1 channels only; A2 +internal token merge; A3 +HiLo-SHSA; A4 +Sparse HR-Anchor.

2) Dataset
- ImageNet-1k (train 1.28M, val 50k). Primary resolution 256 (random resize-crop to 256). Optional 384 scaling to probe s=4/6.

3) Models/Variants
- Baseline: SHViT-S4@256.
- A1: +Channel co-budgeting only (learn and freeze TopK indices; QKV over selected pdim; no token merge; no HiLo; no anchors).
- A2: A1 + SHSA-internal token merge at fixed N′ per mode.
- A3: A2 + HiLo-SHSA.
- A4: A3 + Sparse HR-Anchor (late fusion once).
- Optional external comparators: ToMe/TPS on full tokens for context (accept dynamic-length sequences). We report separately and mark “not static-shape”.

4) Budgets/Modes (exported)
- fast: (pdim≈0.18C, r_merge≈0.35N)
- balanced: (pdim≈0.22C, r_merge≈0.20N)
- max-acc: (pdim≈0.26C, r_merge=0)
- HiLo ratios: α stage2=0.6, stage3=0.7; local window s=2 at 256.
- Anchors: K=8, pdim_a≈0.05C; residual scale 0.5.

5) Training Protocol
- Optimizer: AdamW, base LR 3e-4 (global batch 256), wd=0.05, cosine schedule, total 320–340 epochs.
- Augmentations: RandAug(9,0.5), random resize-crop to 256, Mixup 0.2, CutMix 0.2, label smoothing 0.1. RandErasing off by default.
- Phase-1 (200 epochs):
  - Overprovision pdim_eff≈1.5×pdim for each stage’s attention branch (1x1 conv expansion if needed).
  - Train ChannelScorer with ST Gumbel-TopK; index-dropout 7%.
  - Sample 2 modes per mini-batch: draw two from {fast, balanced, maxacc}. Forward both, share weights, average CE loss. Add inter-mode consistency loss 0.025 on pre-proj logits (KL or L2).
  - Regularizers: coverage/diversity loss from ChannelUsageEMA per stage; small L2 (1e-4) on pre_norm outputs for BN stability.
  - HR-Anchor curriculum: for the variants using anchors, start with random K for 5 epochs, then linearly anneal to saliency Top-K by epoch 15.
- Freeze (after phase-1):
  - Compute EMA hist scores of selected channels per stage across 10k mini-batches; pick TopK and store as nn.Buffers in each stage’s SHSA module; disable scorer in eval.
  - Fix N′ per mode (derived from r_merge) and HiLo params.
- Phase-2 (120–140 epochs):
  - Train with frozen channel indices and fixed budgets per mode. Optionally apply KD: treat max-acc as teacher, distill fast/balanced (hard distill weight 0.5, DeiT recipe).
  - Short BN recalibration per mode: run 1–2k batches without gradient to stabilize BN stats; store per-mode running stats snapshots.

Reference training loop and mode sampling

```python
def train_phase(model, dataloader, optimizer, scaler, modes, epoch, phase, kd_teacher=None):
    model.train()
    for i, (imgs, targets) in enumerate(dataloader):
        imgs, targets = imgs.cuda(non_blocking=True), targets.cuda(non_blocking=True)
        optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
        mode_a, mode_b = np.random.choice(list(modes.keys()), size=2, replace=False)
        loss_total = 0.0
        logits_a = model(imgs, mode=mode_a, training_phase=(phase==1))
        logits_b = model(imgs, mode=mode_b, training_phase=(phase==1))
        ce_a = F.cross_entropy(logits_a, targets)
        ce_b = F.cross_entropy(logits_b, targets)
        loss = 0.5*(ce_a+ce_b)
        # Inter-mode consistency on pre-proj logits if available
        if hasattr(model, 'preproj_logits'):
            l_cons = 0.025 * F.mse_loss(model.preproj_logits[mode_a], model.preproj_logits[mode_b])
            loss = loss + l_cons
        # KD for phase-2 fast/balanced
        if phase==2 and kd_teacher is not None:
            with torch.no_grad():
                tlogits = kd_teacher(imgs, mode='maxacc', training_phase=False)
            loss_kd = 0.5 * F.cross_entropy(logits_a, tlogits.argmax(-1)) + \
                      0.5 * F.cross_entropy(logits_b, tlogits.argmax(-1))
            loss = loss + 0.5 * loss_kd
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
```

BN recalibration per mode

```python
@torch.no_grad()
def bn_recalibrate(model, dataloader, mode, steps=1500):
    model.train()  # update BN stats only
    it = iter(dataloader)
    for _ in range(steps):
        try:
            imgs, _ = next(it)
        except StopIteration:
            it = iter(dataloader)
            imgs, _ = next(it)
        imgs = imgs.cuda(non_blocking=True)
        _ = model(imgs, mode=mode, training_phase=False)
    model.eval()
```

6) Inference/Export
- Export three static artifacts (fast/balanced/max-acc) with frozen channel indices and fixed N′, HiLo, K anchors.
- ONNX export for server backend (K-sim pairing): opset 13+, dynamic indexing allowed but tensor shapes remain constant; no control-flow nodes.
- CoreML for mobile backend (Morton pairing): precomputed merge indices as constants ensure fully static graphs.

ONNX export snippet

```python
def export_onnx(model, mode, out_path, opset=13):
    model.eval()
    dummy = torch.randn(1, 3, 256, 256).cuda()
    with torch.no_grad():
        torch.onnx.export(model, (dummy, mode, False), out_path,
                          input_names=['input', 'mode', 'trainflag'],
                          output_names=['logits'],
                          opset_version=opset, do_constant_folding=True,
                          dynamic_axes={'input': {0: 'batch'}})
    import onnx
    m = onnx.load(out_path)
    from onnxsim import simplify
    m_simp, check = simplify(m)
    onnx.save(m_simp, out_path.replace('.onnx', '_simp.onnx'))
```

CoreML export (mobile backend)

```python
import coremltools as ct

def export_coreml(model, mode, out_path):
    model.eval()
    ex = torch.randn(1, 3, 256, 256)
    traced = torch.jit.trace(model, (ex, mode, False))
    mlmodel = ct.convert(traced, inputs=[ct.ImageType(name='input', shape=ex.shape)])
    mlmodel.save(out_path)
```

7) Metrics
- Accuracy: ImageNet-1k val Top-1.
- Efficiency: FLOPs (fvcore or ptflops), params, peak memory, throughput (images/s), latency p50/p95 on RTX 3090-class GPU, and 8-core CPU.
- Static-shape deployability: ONNX export success; ORT/CoreML compile; count of control-flow ops=0; latency variance across 100 random inputs <=3%.
- Ablations: delta accuracy and FLOPs from A1→A2→A3→A4, per mode.
